claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you a few questions about your child.

**First question: How old is your child?**

The eligibility depends on their age:
- **Under 16**: Children under 16 generally qualify (with some conditions)
- **16-19**: Young people aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Generally not eligible for Child Benefit

Please tell me your child's age (or if you have multiple children, we can go through them one at a time).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - Alex, who is 18 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 18 years old.

Since Alex is 18 (which falls in the 16-19 age range), they may still qualify for Child Benefit if they meet certain education or training requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to understand Alex's current education or training situation.

**For young people aged 16-19, there are different scenarios:**

1. **Currently in approved education or training** - Alex is actively enrolled in an approved course
2. **Recently left education** - Alex has left education or training within the last 20 weeks
3. **Neither** - Alex is not in education/training and left more than 20 weeks ago

**Which of these applies to Alex?**
- Is Alex currently in education or training?
- Or has Alex recently left education or training (within the last 20 weeks)?
- Or neither of these?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex isn't in education or training anymore. They finished school a while back - it's been well over 20 weeks now. They're not doing any courses at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that. Since Alex is 18 years old, not currently in education or training, and left education more than 20 weeks ago, let me navigate to the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Result

I'm sorry to inform you that based on the information provided, **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

### Why Alex doesn't qualify:

For young people aged 16-19 to qualify for Child Benefit, they must be in **approved education or training**. Since Alex:
- Is 18 years old
- Is not currently in education or training
- Left education more than 20 weeks ago

They do not meet the qualifying criteria for Child Benefit.

### Important information:

**Child Benefit rules state that:**
- Children under 16 automatically qualify
- Young people aged 16-19 can qualify **only if** they are in approved education or training
- If a 16-17 year old leaves education, benefit can continue for up to 20 weeks if they register with careers services or armed services
- Once a child turns 20, Child Benefit stops regardless of their circumstances

### What you could consider:

If Alex were to return to approved education or training (such as A-levels, further education college, or approved training courses), you could become eligible for Child Benefit again while they're studying, up until they turn 20.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit eligibility, or do you have any questions about this decision?

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and is not currently in approved education or training. Alex left education more than 20 weeks ago. According to the Child Benefit rules, young people aged 16-19 can only qualify if they are in approved education or training, or if they have recently left education (within the last 20 weeks). Since Alex does not meet either of these requirements, they do not qualify as a 'qualifying child' for Child Benefit purposes.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (aged 18), who does not meet the qualifying criteria for Child Benefit as they are not in approved education or training and left education over 20 weeks ago.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.reason: No qualifying child under 16, or under 20 in approved education/training
response.guidance: Child must be under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. If child just turned 20, benefit stops. If 16-17 year old leaves education, benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with careers service or armed services.

According to the Child Benefit rules, young people aged 16-19 can only qualify if they are in approved education or training, or if they have recently left education (within the last 20 weeks). Since Alex does not meet either of these requirements, they do not qualify as a 'qualifying child' for Child Benefit purposes.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (aged 18), who does not meet the qualifying criteria for Child Benefit as they are not in approved education or training and left education over 20 weeks ago.
